coredata.dc:title: Development of mucoadhesive Timolol loaded chitosan-nanocomposite to treat glaucoma
coredata.dc:creator: Kumara, B.N.
Shambhu, R.
Shim, Yoon-Bo
Nirmal, Jayabalan
Prasad, K. Sudhakara
coredata.dc:description: Timolol Maleate is an aqueous soluble β-blocker antiglaucoma drug used to suppress intraocular pressure. Several commercially available ocular formulations are not effective in delivering to the target site due to their water-soluble property and low mucoadhesiveness. Hence, there is a requirement for a highly mucoadhesive drug-loaded nanocomposite to suppress intraocular pressure with enhanced bioavailability. Herein, we have prepared a mucoadhesive Timolol-loaded graphene quantum dot-chitosan-nanocomposite to treat glaucoma in response to lysozyme, secreted in the tear fluid. The as-prepared nanocomposite has been characterized through high resolution-transmission electron microscopic, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic, X-ray diffraction, and Fourier transform infrared spectral studies. The nanocomposite showed 93.74 % encapsulation efficiency with a loading capacity of 7.73 %. Further, 89.26 %, 95.62 %, and 99.29 % of drug release were observed from the nanocomposite in the presence of 1, 1.5, and 2 mg/mL of lysozyme. The mucoadhesion property has been confirmed by the increment in the particle size, fluorescence spectral variations, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic studies in the presence of mucin nanoparticles of size 291 nm. Interestingly, mucoadhesion has been demonstrated by pointing to the quenching in the luminescence of mucin. Further, in vitro biocompatibility assay on human corneal epithelial cells showed ≥80 % cell viability. Hence, this study offers the utilization of naturally secreting enzymes for drug delivery applications instead of uncontrolled pH and temperature-triggered releases.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Timolol Maleate is an aqueous soluble β-blocker antiglaucoma drug used to suppress intraocular pressure. Several commercially available ocular formulations are not effective in delivering to the target site due to their water-soluble property and low mucoadhesiveness. Hence, there is a requirement for a highly mucoadhesive drug-loaded nanocomposite to suppress intraocular pressure with enhanced bioavailability. Herein, we have prepared a mucoadhesive Timolol-loaded graphene quantum dot-chitosan-nanocomposite to treat glaucoma in response to lysozyme, secreted in the tear fluid. The as-prepared nanocomposite has been characterized through high resolution-transmission electron microscopic, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic, X-ray diffraction, and Fourier transform infrared spectral studies. The nanocomposite showed 93.74 % encapsulation efficiency with a loading capacity of 7.73 %. Further, 89.26 %, 95.62 %, and 99.29 % of drug release were observed from the nanocomposite in the presence of 1, 1.5, and 2 mg/mL of lysozyme. The mucoadhesion property has been confirmed by the increment in the particle size, fluorescence spectral variations, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic studies in the presence of mucin nanoparticles of size 291 nm. Interestingly, mucoadhesion has been demonstrated by pointing to the quenching in the luminescence of mucin. Further, in vitro biocompatibility assay on human corneal epithelial cells showed ≥80 % cell viability. Hence, this study offers the utilization of naturally secreting enzymes for drug delivery applications instead of uncontrolled pH and temperature-triggered releases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Eye, being the most sensitive organ in the human body possesses blood ocular barriers which form an obstacle in the delivery of drugs in the ocular region and thus lack target site action. [1]. Continuous eye blinks, rapid lacrimal secretion, nonproductive absorption, transient residence time, and reflex lachrymation are the key factors that play against ocular therapeutics [2]. Due to the above-said factors, only 1–5 % of the applied dosage is reaching the cornea [3]. However, patients are adapted to the frequent administration of eye drops or ocular suspensions to manage ocular disorders. On the other hand, frequent application of ocular drops results in several consequences such as redness, nausea, swelling, etc. Several ocular disorders affect the visual impairment of human beings. Glaucoma is among the ocular disorder, found life-threatening and therefore it is also called the ‘silent thief of sight’ [3].

In general, glaucoma is an irreversible disorder caused by variations in intraocular pressure (IOP), it further leads to vision loss due to the death of retinal ganglion cells (RGCs) [3]. To decrease the IOP, several eye drops are available in the market. Among them, TM is an anti-glaucoma β-blocker drug used to suppress IOP. When comes to the mechanism of action, it decreases the production of aqueous humor by blocking the sympathetic nerve endings in the ciliary epithelium. Hence, TM is considered for administration due to its tolerance, rapid onset action, minimum side effect, and aqueous solubility of the drug. However, it is associated with certain limitations such as short residence at the cornea followed by bioavailability [4]. Similar to TM, several β-blocker drugs are commercially available such as carteolol, betaxolol, levobunolol, and metoprolol which are front-line treatments for glaucoma. In an ocular drug delivery system, maintaining the bioavailability of the delivered drug for a longer time is still challenging. In addition, commercially available eye drops and ocular suspensions are lacking in addressing the present concerns due to the hydrophilic nature of drugs followed by the less mucoadhesion of the compositions [5]. Similar to other eye drops and ocular formulations, TM eye drops also have drawbacks with short residence and deprived ocular permeability. Due to short residence, the administered drug will flush out through lacrimal fluid which causes side effects such as bradycardia, hypotension, and bronchospasm at a systemic level, redness, dryness, and swelling [6]. Therefore, the recent trend of research is focussing on improving the ocular bioavailability of the drug by providing a mucoadhesive nature.

Mucoadhesion is the key factor to improve drug delivery by prolonged contact time, enhanced drug absorption, bypassing first-pass metabolism, and preventing drug loss during target site action [7]. Especially, mucoadhesive gels and nanocomposites enhance the target site action by binding to the mucus layers and it depends on the polymer. Among the available polymers, chitosan (CS) is found to be a very versatile material due to its extensive features [8]. In brief, CS is a cationic polysaccharide utilized in most biomedical applications due to its biocompatible, antimicrobial, antibacterial, anti-cancer, and anti-oxidant nature. Further, it is also supporting corneal penetration, prolonged residence, and drug retention capacity including high biodegradability [3]. Therefore, in the present work, we have utilized CS as a polymer for sustained drug delivery applications by considering its above-mentioned factors in addition to Lysozyme (Lyz)-responsive hydrolysis. Lyz is an enzyme, abundantly available in human lacrimal fluid, breast milk, sow milk, and saliva. Hence, Lyz could be used as a stimulus for ocular drug delivery applications. In the recent literature, to evaluate the ocular penetration and to analyze the drug loading studies, several fluorescent dyes have been explored including fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots, and toxic staining agents [9,10]. The presence of these dyes for a longer time in the ocular part may cause redness, swelling, and dryness. Therefore, there is a requirement for highly biocompatible, fluorescent material to track drug delivery. The low-cost, ease of synthesis, biocompatibility, and aqueous solubility nature of fluorescent nanomaterial could open a new avenue for drug delivery applications.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanomaterials employed in various biomedical applications including bioimaging, drug delivery, gene delivery, sensing, catalysis, optoelectronics, etc. due to their photoluminescence (PL) nature, biocompatibility, aqueous solubility, band gap structures and multi-functionalization ability [11,12]. A plethora of literature has been discussed about the utilization of GQDs for drug loading confirmation by pointing to their fluorescence variations. In addition, Pan and his research group thoroughly worked on reverse-switching the PL of carbon nanomaterial by counter-ion interaction and UV treatment [13,14]. Very recently, our work explored the outstanding PL property of GQDs by embedding them in the drug-loaded nanocomposite for drug delivery tracking applications by a simple PL spectral technique [15]. It is noteworthy to mention that, the utilization of the PL feature of GQDs could be an add-on advantage in determining the mucoadhesiveness by pointing its emission variations in the presence of mucin nanoparticles using UV light (365 nm) and emission spectral studies.

Therefore, we have reported the preparation of a water-soluble drug, TM-loaded CS nanocomposite with improved mucoadhesiveness to further utilize for glaucoma management. The optical and surface morphological characterizations have been analyzed by using Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), particle size, zeta potential, high resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies. Drug release studies have been conducted in the presence of various concentrations of ocular enzyme, Lyz. The mucoadhesive nature has been confirmed by particle size analysis, fluorescence spectral variations due to the presence of PL GQDs, and FT-IR studies. Interestingly, mucoadhesion has been demonstrated by pointing to the quenching in the luminescence of mucin nanoparticles in the presence of caged drug nanocomposite. Further, in vitro biological studies suggest the high biocompatibility of the prepared nanocomposite against Human corneal epithelial (HCE) cells. Moreover, the developed formulation could stand out as a better therapeutic option to treat glaucoma by reducing the problems associated with mucoadhesiveness.

The Graphene oxide (GO, 2 mg/mL), Timolol maleate, Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), Branched polyethyleneimine (PEI, branched, average Mw-800 by LS, average Mn ~600 by GPC), Succinic acid (SA), 1-ethyl-3-[3-dimethylamino propyl]-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS), Chitosan (CS, MW: 50,000–190,000 Da (based on viscosity), 75–85 % deacetylated), Lysozyme (Lyz, ≥40,000 units/mg protein) from egg white, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), Acridine orange (AO), Acetic acid, Ethidium bromide (EB), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) from Sigma-Aldrich chemicals Private Ltd. Further, Hydrochloric acid (HCl), Sodium chloride (NaCl), Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), Potassium chloride (KCl), Calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2.2H2O), and Mucin (from the porcine stomach, Type II, bound sialic acid, ≤1.2 %, M2378) from sigma-Aldrich chemicals private limited and Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) from Merck specialties private limited. In addition to this, Nitric acid (HNO3) was procured from HI Media. Quinine sulfate dihydrate (99 %) was purchased from Loba Chemie private limited. Dialysis membrane was obtained from spectrum labs (MWCM: 0.5–1 kDa). The procured chemicals were used as such without any further purifications. The cell culture reagents were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd.

The optical spectral studies and drug quantifications were conducted by using UV–Visible spectroscopy, UV-1800 Shimadzu UV spectrophotometer, Japan. Further, to evaluate the emission intensity and quantum yield, the emission Spectroscopy instrument (model F-2700 FL Spectrophotometer 4J1-0004) was employed. To check the functional groups, Fourier Transform- Infrared spectroscopic (FT-IR) investigations were performed with the attenuated total reflectance mode (FTIR-ATR, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). A High-Resolution- Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) instrument was used to determine the surface morphological structures of the as-prepared composite. The X-ray diffraction (XRD), RIGAKU ULTIMA-IV with Scintillation counter, and dTex detector were used to evaluate the phase identification of the prepared nanocomposite. In addition to this, particle size (DLS) and zeta potential analysis were investigated by the Malvern Zetasizer Ultra instrument. The chemical structure was characterized by 1H NMR (400 MHz, JNM-ECZ400/L1) on a JOEL High resolution Multinuclear FT-NMR spectrometer. The ZOE, BioRad, microplate reader (FluoSTAR Omega, BMG Labtech), PrimoVert, Carl Zeiss, and inverted phase-contrast microscope (Zeiss) instruments were employed to analyze the in vitro biological studies. The KinetDS (version 3.0) and the DDSolver® software were employed for the mathematical modelling and GraphPad Prism 8 software for statistical analysis.

To prepare the TM-embedded nanocomposite, primarily, GQDs were prepared and characterized thoroughly. Further, it was passivated by using polyethylene imine (PEI) and succinic acid (SA) to improvise the functional groups as reported previously [15]. Separately, 2 % (w/v) CS (Low molecular weight, LMW) was prepared by adding 2 % (v/v) acetic acid (AA) and adjusting the pH to 5.5 to achieve good encapsulation efficiency (EE). The required amount of TM (10 mg/mL) was weighed and added to a beaker containing CS and passivated GQD in the presence of EDC/NHS. The as-obtained composite was allowed to remove the unbounded drug. Before doing this, the pH was readjusted to 7.0–7.4 and the nanocomposite was dialyzed against 0.5–1 kDa molecular weight cutoff membrane (MWCM) to remove the unbounded drug. The quantification of the drug was analyzed by using a UV–Vis spectrometer at 294 nm. As a part of maintaining the sterility, before using the materials for the preparation of the drug nanocomposite, they have been filtered using 0.22 μm and 0.45 μm syringe filters followed by dialysis using 0.5–1 kDa membrane. Further, the developed nanocomposite has been exposed to UV radiation (100–280 nm wavelength) before conducting the in vitro cell culture studies [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of surface morphology by HR-TEM analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The HR-TEM analysis has been performed to determine the surface morphology of the drug nanocomposite. For the analysis, a drop of the graphene quantum dots (GQD) and drug nanocomposite sample was placed on a copper grid and dried. The morphology of the developed samples was analyzed using HR-TEM (HR-TEM, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size, zeta potential, and polydispersity index studies
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis such as particle size, zeta potential, and polydispersity index (PDI) of the developed nanocomposite has been determined by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique using Malvern Zeta Sizer, Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK. For the experiment, 1 mL of the sample was used and the analysis was performed at room temperature [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 1H-nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) characterization of nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The 1H-Nuclear magnetic resonance spectra (1H NMR) have been carried out to identify the structure of the developed materials. To check the same, the drug, Timolol (TM), and TM-loaded nanocomposite (freeze-dried) were taken and dissolved in DMSO‑d 6 (5 mg/0.6 mL) for analysis. Further, the chemical structure was characterized by 1H NMR (400 MHz, JNM-ECZ400/L1) on a JOEL High resolution Multinuclear FT-NMR spectrometer.

For the analysis, Quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, Φ =0.54) has been considered as a standard to analyze the QY (Φ) of the synthesized photoluminescent GQDs and the developed nanocomposite. The QY (Φ) was determined by using the below-mentioned equation [18].
Fluorescence quantum yield
Φ
=
Φ st
I x
/
I t
A x
/
A t
η 2
x
/
η 2
t
where ‘Φ’ refers to photoluminescence quantum yield, ‘I’ is integrated emission intensity, ‘A’ refers to absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and ‘η’ is the refractive index of the solvent used. The subscripts mentioned as ‘st’ and ‘x’ are standard (st) with known photoluminescence quantum yield and the sample (t), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mucoadhesion studies by particle size analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The mucoadhesiveness studies have been performed to evaluate the in vitro adhesion of the nanocomposite towards the mucin layer. The studies have been conducted by using previously available reports [19,20]. The increases in the size of the nanocomposite in the presence of mucin suggest the mucoadhesive nature. Hence, commercially available mucin (1 % (m/v)) was hydrated in demineralized water at 4 °C for 12 h. Further, the mucin solution was diluted with 0.1 M PBS and adjusted the pH with 1 M NaOH. The prepared solution was ultrasonicated for 30 min to reduce the size followed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 20 min. The obtained supernatant solution was filtered using a 0.45 μm filter membrane. To investigate mucoadhesiveness, mucin of <500 nm was considered for the investigation. Further, equal volumes of TM-loaded nanocomposites and the mucin particles were mixed by vertexing for a min, and analysis was performed by the DLS technique.

For the experiment, 0.5 mL of mucin nanoparticles were taken in the quartz cuvette and recorded the emission intensity. Next, TM-loaded nanocomposite (5–50 μL) was added sequentially to mucin and recorded the difference in the spectral peaks. The graph was plotted for intensity versus wavelength.

The mucoadhesion study was carried out by using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy-attenuated total reflectance (FT-IR-ATR) mode by pointing out its absorption intensity variations upon complex formation due to interactions [21,22]. The analysis was carried out for mucin, TM-loaded nanocomposite, mucin-TM-loaded nanocomposite-1 min, and mucin-TM-loaded nanocomposite-5 min. The intensities were recorded and differences in the absorption of amide I/amide II bonds were analyzed.

The entrapment efficiency (EE) and loading capacity (LC) of TM were analyzed by using the UV–Visible spectral method at 294 nm (λmax) [23,24]. To calculate both EE and LC, the below-mentioned formula was used [25].
Entrapment efficiency
EE
%
=
Total amount of drug present
/ Total amount of drug added ) × 100
Loading capacity
LC
%
=
Total amount of
TM
present / Total amount of nanocomposite
× 100

The drug release studies were performed by using simulated tear fluid (STF). To prepare, in 1-liter beaker containing double distilled water, 6.78 g/L of NaCl, 2.18 g/L of NaHCO3, 1.38 g/L of KCl, and 0.084 g/L of CaCl2.2H2O were added and mixed thoroughly [26]. Further, to adjust the pH to 7.4, 2 M HCl and NaOH were used.

To study in vitro drug release from the nanocomposite, various concentrations of Lyz (1 mg/mL, 1.5 mg/mL, 2 mg/mL)-treated nanocomposite (2.5 mL) are loaded into 0.5–1 kDa MWCM followed by placing into a beaker containing 30 mL of STF (pH -7.4) at 37 °C with medium stirring to maintain the physiological conditions. Next, 2 mL of the dialysate was collected from the beaker at pre-determined time points such as 1, 2, 6, 12, 24, 36, 48, 60, and 72 h and replenished by the same amount of STF to maintain the constant sink volume. The collected samples were kept in a 2–8 °C refrigerator until the analysis. Primarily, calibration of the TM was performed at different concentrations from 0 to 200 μg/mL and calculated the regression coefficient (R2 = 0.9993). Further, collected samples were quantified by UV–Visible spectroscopy at 294 nm [23]. In addition, quantification of drug release was performed by using UV–Visible spectroscopy including EE and LC using the photometric method. Further, mathematical modelling of the obtained drug release data was performed by using Kinet_DS (version 3.0) and the DDSolver®. Further, the in vitro drug release data fit into various standard kinetic equations such as zero-order, first-order, Higuchi, Koresmayer-Peppas, and Hixson-Crowell models [27,28]. The selection of the model suggesting the drug release pattern from the drug-loaded nanocomposite was identified by considering the coefficient of regression (R2) obtained from the models.

The phenotype of the in vitro human corneal epithelial (HCE) cells replicates the nature of in vivo ocular tissues. Hence, to evaluate the toxicity of the prepared nanocomposite, we have considered HCE cells and it has been procured from National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS), Pune, India. To culture, Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM): Ham's F12 supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % antibiotic-antimycotic solution was utilized. Similar to other cell lines, HCE cells were kept at 37 °C under 5 % CO2 and subcultured upon attaining 70–80 % confluence. The cells were used for the studies after 3 passages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assessment using methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay
ce:section.ce:para:
To analyze the biocompatibility of the as-prepared nanocomposite, a cytotoxicity assay (MTT) has been performed on cultured HCE cells [29]. In 96 well plate, cells were seeded at a density of 5000 cells/well and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2. The TM-embedded nanocomposite and Lyz-treated TM-embedded nanocomposite were treated to cells at various concentrations (20–100 μg/mL), followed by an incubation of 24 h. After incubation, spent media was removed and 1 mg/mL of 100 μL of MTT solution was added followed by incubation for 4 h. The absorbance of formazan crystals was analyzed at 570 nm using a multimode microplate reader, before that, they were solubilized in 100 μL of DMSO. To calculate the percentage of viability, the below-mentioned formula was used.
Cell viability
%
=
Optical density of the cells treated with samples / Optical density of the control
× 100 .

The live/dead staining assay, that is AO/EB analysis was conducted to understand the viability and apoptotic stages of cultured HCE cells [30]. The HCE cells were seeded to 24 well plates with a seeding density of 10,000 cells/well and incubated at 37 °C under 5 % CO2 for 24 h. The TM-loaded nanocomposite and Lyz-treated nanocomposite were added at various concentrations in three different concentrations such as 20, 60, and 100 μg/mL, and further incubated for 24 h. Next, spent media from the wells was removed and cells were washed with 1xPBS. After the required washing, staining was carried out using AO-EB (2 μg/mL, 1:1) for 15 min at 37 °C under dark conditions. The excess stain was removed and cells were washed with PBS twice. Further, cells were overlaid with PBS and imaged under the green and red channels of a fluorescence imager. Theoretically, nuclei with green fluorescence were considered viable cells, and cells with red fluorescence or greenish-yellow were considered an apoptotic stage of cells under the merge channel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of in vitro morphological changes
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological changes of the HCE cells were observed before and after treatment with TM-loaded nanocomposite microscopically. The cultured HCE cells were seeded onto 6 wells plate and incubated to grow. Further, the cells were treated with different concentrations of the nanocomposite and observed morphological variations upon 24 h of exposure. Later, the images were captured under 20× magnifications using an inverted phase-contrast microscope.

The statistical analysis was plotted for in vitro biocompatibility and mucoadhesion studies, and calculated by taking mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). One-way ANOVA analysis of variance A (Tukey's multiple comparisons test) using GraphPad Prism 8 software.

HR-TEM analysis was performed to investigate the surface morphology of the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and drug-loaded nanocomposite. It has been noticed that GQDs appeared as a uniform dispersion of spherical shape (Fig. 1A) and demonstrated the lattice spacing distance of 0.22 nm, which was intended to be for the (100) lattice constant of the plane of graphite (Fig. 1A inset image). The obtained results also confirm that GQDs are comprised of few graphene layers and indicate the ultrasmall structure with an average size of 3.82 ± 0.63 nm [15]. Further, under 200 nm and 50 nm magnifications, aggregated drug composite was found in the CS matrix (Fig. 1B-C). In addition to this, the embedding of the drug in the CS matrix could endow sustained release for a longer time, and morphological arrangements are similar to the reported study [31,32]. A nanosphere-shaped TM morphology was reported by Shokry et al. in gelatin nanoparticles in the presence of a 1:2 ratio of amine and glutaraldehyde (NH2: GA) [5]. In general, CS is a highly mucoadhesive material for ocular drug delivery applications and has an affinity towards the mucus layer of the cornea [15,33,34]. Even, Tan and the group discussed the preparation of TM-encapsulated CS-loaded liposomal formulation to treat glaucoma wherein the spherical-shaped particles confirm the presence of TM [19]. Prior to the current study, we have analyzed and reported the optical and surface morphological characterizations of the synthesized PL GQDs and passivated GQDs [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR spectral studies of TM-loaded nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectral studies have been conducted to evaluate the presence of TM in the nanocomposite. In particular to the TM drug, the peaks found at 3366 cm−1 and 3246 cm−1 are responsible for NH stretching, and -OH stretching vibrations. Further, CO stretching vibration peaks appeared at 1626 cm−1, CH bending vibration was observed at 1386 cm−1, and 1540 cm−1 and 1459 cm−1 are responsible for CC aromatic rings of the drug [35]. Similar peaks associated with the drug were observed in the TM-loaded nanocomposite. In brief, vibrational peaks at 3238 cm−1 and 3360 cm−1 represents the occurrence of -OH and -NH functional groups. The peak that appeared at 1635 cm−1 shows the occurrence of carboxylic groups associated with the TM and CS structures as shown in Fig. S1. Since GQDs and CS are rich in oxygen functional groups, we have observed the peaks at 1546 cm−1, 1435 cm−1, 1378 cm−1, 1170 cm−1, and 1048 cm−1 are corresponding to aromatic rings, -NH, CC stretching, C-O/C-O-C, and CH bending vibrations, respectively as reported [15]. Therefore, the FT-IR spectral study confirms the presence of TM in the developed nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray photoelectronic spectral (XPS) and 1H NMR studies
ce:section.ce:para: The XPS analysis was performed to evaluate the percentage and atomic share of each element present in the drug-loaded nanocomposite. In detail, from the survey scan analysis of nanocomposite (Fig. S2), the presence of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p at 287.71 eV, 401.73 eV, 535.23 eV, and 163.98 eV has been observed. Further, from the high-resolution deconvoluted spectra, we have noticed the presence of several functional groups on the surface of the nanocomposite. As reported in our previous work for the passivated GQD, elemental shares of 75.01 %, 18.95 %, and 0.65 % for C1s, O1s, and N1s having the binding energy at 284.51 eV, 532.51 eV, and 401.51 eV was seen [15]. In the case of TM-loaded nanocomposite, the elements C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p have existed with 66.64 %, 7.42 %, 25.83 %, and 0.1 %, respectively. Hence, the XPS study confirms that the increment in N and O atomic shares in comparison to passivated GQD is due to the loading of the drug. In addition to this, TM has sulfur in it, therefore, we have found 0.1 %.

The functional groups formed on the nanocomposite upon drug loading have been confirmed by high-resolution deconvoluted spectral studies. In the case of the deconvoluted C1s spectrum, the peaks were found at 285.29 eV, 285.62 eV, 287.99 eV, and 289.65 eV respectively. These binding energies (BE) are responsible for the presence of C-C/C=C, C-O/C-O-C, CN, and C=O/COOH functional groups (Fig. 2A). In the case of N1s, the peaks present at 399.59 eV, 402.06 eV, and 403.65 eV represent the formation of amino N, graphitic nitrogen, and Nitro functional groups (Fig. 2B). When it comes to O1s, we have observed the peaks at 532.09 eV, 534.38 eV, and 537.71 eV continuously for C-O-C/C-O, C-OH, and adsorbed O2/C respectively (Fig. 2C). Further, the O/C ratio of 0.38 was reported which is comparably more than the passivated GQD (0.25). The increment in the O/C ratio confirms the loading of TM in the nanocomposite. In the atomic percentage share of C1s, O1s, and N1s of passivated GQD and TM-loaded nanocomposite, we could notice 0.88 % varied ‘C’, 1.36 % increased ‘O’ and 11.41 % elevated ‘N’. Therefore, the obtained XPS spectral data confirm the loading TM in the nanocomposite.

Further, 1H NMR spectra of TM reveal distinct peaks (Fig. S3a), and the total proton count corresponds to the drug material's structure [36]. In the case of the TM-loaded nanocomposite spectra (Fig. S3b), we observe a singlet at a chemical shift (δ) value of 1.23 ppm representing the tertiary butyl proton, along with identical peaks for the alkenyl proton and the acid (-COOH) at approximately 6 ppm and 8.2 ppm, respectively, as seen in the drug spectra. However, the peaks within the range of 2.0–5.0 ppm demonstrate merging with CS and other composite materials present in the drug composite spectra.

The XRD is a basic analytical technique used to identify and characterize the phase by pointing out its diffraction patterns. In the present work, the GQDs obtained from GO showed a highly intense peak at 33.34° responsible for the high crystallinity of the material. In addition to this, the peaks were found at 20.33°, 29.26°, 30.21°, 35.12°, and 39.76° respectively [37] (Fig. 3 ). The raw graphite powder exhibited a characteristic peak at 26.42° responsible for the (002) plane with a d-spacing of 0.366 nm [38]. Similarly, we have got the peak at 29.26°, the variations are expected due to the carbonizations under oxidizing agents and the formation of oxygen functionalities and edge structures of the GQDs. The GO peaks were not observed at 10.2° due to the exfoliation of layers while forming GQDs [39]. Further, the pure CS showed broad peaks from 10 to 30° with the peak centered at 20.94° with a shoulder peak at 16.22° responsible for the pure CS with the planes of (220) and (110) respectively [40]. The presence of intermolecular and intramolecular hydrogen bonding is responsible for the rigid structure of CS. Similar results have been observed by Aziz et al., who reported the presence of CS peaks at 15.1° and 20.9° [41]. The drug, TM showed intense peaks in various regions from 10 to 30° and especially high intensity at 20.17° responsible for the crystallinity of the drug, and recent reports showed the highly intense TM peak at 19.47° [16,42]. In Fig. 3, similar peaks were found in the TM-loaded nanocomposite with the presence of a less intense peak at 10.1° responsible for the occurrence of TM, and also a broad peak from 15°-38° is extensive for CS occurrence. It is very interesting to note that, the peaks of CS are from 15°-30°, however, extended broad peaks were observed for TM-loaded nanocomposite, which is expected for GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential studies
ce:section.ce:para: The dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique has been performed to evaluate the particle size of the nanocomposite. The obtained TM-loaded nanocomposite showed a size of 503.1 nm (Intensity v/s Size) with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.518 (Fig. S4). The PDI provides the particle size distribution and it is a crucial parameter for the formulation to consider for ocular applications. The expected range of PDI for the CS-derived formulations is from 0.17 to 1, and >0.7 are referred to as broadly distributed particles [34] and the obtained data from our study is in the agreeable range of CS-based nanoformulations [43]. Further, the zeta potential of the nanocomposite is found to be +29.1 ± 0.41. The occurrence of a positively charged surface influences greater mucoadhesiveness property, which has been thoroughly explained in the next section by performing particle size analysis, emission spectral studies, and FT-IR studies.

Theoretically, the corneal region is having mucin, which can bind with positively charged material when in contact. Similarly, it can also hold the ocular suspensions, eye drops, or nanocomposite to deliver the drug for a longer time. Mucins are macromolecules having a weight ranging from 10 to 40 MDa [44]. It is usually secreted in epithelial goblet cells and submucosal glands. Mucin is comprised of a repeated number of serine, proline, and threonine with heavily dense O-linked oligosaccharides (OLS) [45]. These oligosaccharide chains are having N-acetylgalactosamine, N-acetylglucosamine, galactose, sialic acid, and fructose, and low amounts of mannose and sulfate as predominant residues. The presence of sialic acid (pKa ~2.6) and sulfate moieties (pKa < 1) on the terminal groups of glycoproteins offers an effective negative charge to the mucin [46]. The mucoadhesive assay is highly recommended for ocular formulations and if the nanocomposite showed a high mucoadhesive behavior, it supports sustainable drug delivery to the anterior region of the eye for a longer period.

Therefore, in the present work, in vitro mucoadhesive study has been performed to evaluate the adhesive behavior of nanocomposites in the presence of mucin nanoparticles of <500 nm size. There are different methods used routinely to investigate adhesive behaviors such as the balance pulley method, turbidimetry, viscometry, small-deformation rheology, zeta potential, particle size analysis, and tensile grip method [47,48]. In the case of zeta potential studies, the negatively charged mucin interacts with positively charged nanocomposite due to the cationic property of CS, which results in the formation of a negatively charged complex due to adherence [49]. Among all, particle size analysis is found easy to describe the adhesive nature of the material.

In the case of particle size analysis, the massive increase in the particle size of the mucin-TM loaded nanocomposite proves the binding ability of the nanocomposite due to possible electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding as shown in Scheme 1 . In brief, upon mixing a similar volume of avg. 291 nm-sized mucin nanoparticles with TM-loaded nanocomposite (503 nm) results in the formation of an avg. 2151 nm-sized complex upon vertexing for a minute (Fig. 4A) similar to the reported study [19,20,50]. In addition to this, the formation of slight turbidity of the nanocomposite was also observed upon mixing both nanocomposite and mucin (Fig. 4B(a-c)). Since PL GQDs have been employed for the development of the drug-loaded nanocomposite, we have performed emission variation studies in the presence of UV light of 365 nm. The enhancement and quenching of the emission due to the mucoadhesion could be easily determined as similar to drug delivery tracking. For the analysis, we have exposed mucin, TM-loaded nanocomposite, and mucin-TM-loaded nanocomposite to UV light and found variations in the emission (Fig. 4B(d-f)). In brief, GQDs exhibits high PL property and upon embedding the drug, lose their PL due to the increased influence of polymer matrices which was believed to afflict a stronger effect on the surface electronic structures, resulting in changes of energy gaps [15]. Therefore, we have observed a similar non-fluorescent nanocomposite in Fig. 4B(a, d) under UV light of wavelength 365 nm. In addition, the photoluminescent-quantum yield (PL-QY) for the fluorescent GQDs is found to be 70.6 % (Φ = 0.706) similar to the reported study for the GQDs with the size of 1–3 nm [18] whereas, TM-loaded nanocomposite is 0 % (Φ = 0) due to the non-fluorescent nature. Hence, PL-QY analysis additionally confirms the non-fluorescent nature of the composite upon drug loading. On the other hand, mucin is found to show fluorescence behavior possibly due to its functional groups (Fig. 4B(b, e)). Interestingly, upon the mixing of both, quenching in the PL of mucin was observed due to the interaction with TM-loaded nanocomposite (Fig. 4B(c, f)). This study also supports the mucoadhesive nature of the TM-loaded nanocomposite. Further, to evaluate the amount of TM-loaded nanocomposite required to quench the PL of the mucin, we have conducted emission spectral studies. The mucin showed excitation-dependent emission spectra (Fig. 4C). To understand the PL quenching upon mucin addition, different volumes (5–50 μL) of TM-loaded nanocomposite were added. It is very interesting to note that, the addition of 50 μL of nanocomposite completely reduces the PL of mucin due to its interactions as shown in Fig. 4D and Scheme 1, which confirms the mucoadhesiveness.

Further, the interactions of mucin and TM-loaded nanocomposite were also confirmed by ATR-FT-IR spectral studies. The ATR-FT-IR is a powerful tool to identify minor changes in the biochemical interactions within the materials. In brief, the mucin showed the CO peak at 1637 cm−1 associated with CO stretching of amide I, 1539 cm−1 responsible for CN stretching amide II, 1464 cm−1 for CH bending, 1330 cm−1 and 1232 cm−1 for amide III, 1045 cm−1 for CO stretching/C-O bending vibrations of carbohydrates, and 984 cm−1 for phosphorylated proteins including 3235 cm−1 and 3370 cm−1 for -NH and -OH functional groups (Fig. 5A). For the FT-IR analysis, the TM-loaded nanocomposite was incubated in mucin solution for 1 and 5 mins and recorded the absorption. Before this, the absorption of mucin was evaluated and it was found to be 0.17 (a.u.). Further, we analyzed the TM-loaded nanocomposite and it was observed as 0.175 (a.u.). Interestingly, the nanocomposite-treated mucin provided an amide I/amide II ratio that was found lower than that of mucin which is 0.157 and 0.152 for 1 and 5 min, respectively (Fig. 5B). The decrease in the absorption of amide I/amide II is due to the protection of peptide bonds in mucin from the interface while mucin strongly interacts with TM-loaded nanocomposite. Moreover, the interaction of cationic-charged CS with sialic acid of mucin through electrostatic interactions results in suppressing the absorption of amide bonds [22,47,51]. Besides, the formation of hydrogen bonding between the -NH2 group of CS and COOH moieties present in the sialic acid of mucin may also result in a decrease in absorption peak. Similar results were obtained by Uthaiwat et al. wherein they added CS-composed gel to mucin followed by the reduction in the amide I/amide II bonds appearing <1 (a.u.) after interaction with mucin [21]. Even, Saiano et al. showed the interaction of mucin with α, β-poly(N-hydroxyethyl)-DL-aspartamide and α, β-poly(aspartylhydrazide) polymer results in the decrease in the amide I/amide II bonds [22]. Hence, the obtained results from the above-said experiments suggest the interaction of mucin with TM-loaded nanocomposite.

The natural enzyme, lysozyme (Lyz) can cleave the β-1,4 glycosidic bonds of CS. Therefore, drug delivery studies have been performed to assess the time-dependent release of TM in the presence of the ocular enzyme (Fig. 6 ). We have plotted the calibration curve for the concentration from 0 to 200 μg/mL and observed the regression coefficient (R2) of 0.999 for TM (Fig. S5) at 294 nm wavelength [24]. For the nanocomposite, 93.74 % of EE with an LC of 7.73 % has been observed.

Further, stimuli responsive release studies were carried out by adding various concentrations of Lyz (1 mg/mL, 1.5 mg/mL, and 2 mg/mL) as described in the previous section by considering the availability of the same in human tears. The TM-loaded nanocomposite released 89.26 % of the loaded drug in the presence of 1 mg/mL of Lyz. In the case of 1.5 mg/mL treated nanocomposite, we noticed the release of 95.62 % of the loaded drug within 72 h of the study. For 2 mg/mL Lyz-treated nanocomposite, drug release was found to be 99.29 %. Interestingly, burst release was not found in the first few hours in different concentrations of Lyz-treated nanocomposite due to the stimuli-responsive nature, after which sustained release was determined. In the 3 different sets, until 60 h of the study, the drug release was gradually increased and after that, it was almost constant. A similar kind of result was reported by Shokry et al., wherein, TM was encapsulated into a gelatin nanoparticle and it showed an efficiency of 74 % [5]. The mean Lyz secretion in the tear fluid is 1.5 ± 0.5 mg/mL [52]. Hence, our nanocomposite could release the drug in the human corneal region for a longer time in response to naturally secreting enzymes.

The drug release kinetics were determined by overlapping the obtained data with various kinetic models. Further, the possible release kinetics was explained by considering the high coefficient of regression (R2) as shown in Table 1 and the equations are summarized in supporting information. The obtained results from the models show that the release kinetics follow zero order and the Korsmeyer-Peppas model, which demonstrates that drug release is independent of the concentration of the drug present, and more than one mechanism is involved in the drug release process possibly by a non-Fickian or anomalous transport mechanism (release exponent, n > 0.45). The obtained kinetics results are on-par with the proposed mechanism in our study that, Lyz is involved in the releasing of the drug by cleaving the β-1,4 glycosidic linkages of CS and also the possible erosion/diffusion of the drug due to the swelling nature [15]. In addition, the release nature is also falling in the Higuchi model, suggesting the probable drug release by a diffusion process. The Hixson-Crowell model displays that the reduction/erosion in the surface area or the decrease in the diameter of the nanocomposite during the release, which can be observed in our study that, the drug release occurs upon the cleavage of the nanocomposite, results in the formation of fragmented CS [53].

The biocompatibility assay has been carried out to understand the non-toxic behavior of the as-prepared composite against HCE cell lines. To perform the experiment, TM-loaded nanocomposite and Lyz-treated nanocomposite has been taken from 20 to 100 μg/mL and treated in the cells. The results found >85 % and 80 % cell viability for TM-loaded and Lyz-treated TM-loaded nanocomposite as shown in Fig. 7 . Similarly, our previous work discussed the compatibility study by taking HCE at the same concentration and it exhibited >80 % cell viability for latanoprost-loaded nanocomposite [15].

Further, a live/dead (AO/EB) stain assay has been conducted to evaluate the stages of apoptosis concerning each concentration in the presence of nanocomposites. It has been noted that TM-loaded nanocomposite (Fig. 7C) and Lyz-treated TM-loaded nanocomposite (Fig. S6) with 20, 60, and 100 μg/mL treated cells did not show any red-stained cells in the red field imaging due to the viability. Therefore, a healthy nucleus can be seen in the nanocomposite-treated cells as similar to control cells. Importantly, even in the merged imaging field, we haven't found any red-stained or yellow-stained cells, which confirms the good compatibility of the developed nanocomposite.

In vitro bright-field imaging study was conducted to evaluate the morphological effect of nanocomposite on the HCE cell lines. If the cells are shrunken and dead, it can be easily observed by bright-field imaging. Therefore, we treated similar concentrations such as 20, 60, and 100 μg/mL of the drug-loaded nanocomposite to HCE cell lines and observed the cells after a day of treatment. Further, the cells were imaged under an inverted microscope and observed the intact morphology of the cells is shown in Fig. S7 due to the compatibility of the nanocomposite. In addition to cytocompatibility and live dead staining assay, in vitro bright field imaging has been considered as one of the morphological assessments to evaluate the compatibility of the prepared nanocomposites.

In the present study, a highly mucoadhesive TM-loaded nanocomposite was successfully prepared and characterized. The surface morphological studies such as HR-TEM, XRD, XPS,1H NMR, and FT-IR studies proved the TM encapsulation. Further, mucoadhesive studies such as particle size analysis, PL spectral, and FT-IR-ATR confirmed the high mucoadhesive nature of the nanocomposite towards the mucin, which replicates the intact mucin layer of the cornea. It is noteworthy to mention that, the quenching in the PL of the mucin nanoparticles in the presence of caged TM-loaded nanocomposite confirms the mucoadhesive nature. In vitro drug release experiments proved that the availability of lysozyme (1.5 ± 0.5 mg/mL) in the human lacrimal fluid hydrolyzes the CS, followed by the release of the drug from the nanocomposite in 72 h of the study. In addition to the above, in vitro biocompatibility, bright field imaging, and AO/EB staining studies against human corneal epithelial cells (HCE) suggest good biocompatibility (≥80 %) of the nanocomposite. Therefore, the developed TM-loaded nanocomposite could be employed for the treatment of glaucoma, however, in vivo studies need to be conducted to evaluate the efficacy of the nanocomposite. Further, the enzyme-responsive drug release strategy could be explored in biomedical implants such as a microneedle, ocular inserts, or contact lenses by employing nanocomposites.

BNK: All Experiments, Morphological studies, Fabrication of nanocomposites, Data analysis, Cell culture experiments, Writing-original draft, Investigation. RS: Writing-review & editing. YBS: XPS analysis; Writing-review & editing. JN: Writing-review & editing. KSP: Conceptualization; Funding acquisition; Supervision; Data analysis, Writing-review & editing; Methodology; Investigation, Project administration.
